title: Decision briefing: Expand automated evals (coverage vs grounding, trace utility, and cost)
summary: Recommendation: Expand automated evals cautiously via one more smaller, highly-instrumented visible batch before broader coverage, with explicit shipping gates for citation support/grounding, capped retries with manifest reporting, and holdout labels kept out of tuning workflows. This addresses reviewer throughput/trace usefulness while preventing regressions where coverage rises but unsupported claims slip into customer-facing recommendations.

claims.text: Reviewers report that broad automated runs increased scored cases but reduced usability: failures were harder to cluster into repeated patterns vs one-offs, slowing review and making prompt changes less actionable; smaller reviewed batches made repeated citation issues easier to group and act on.

Coverage can improve even as grounding regresses, so citation support should be an explicit shipping gate: candidates can look better in aggregate yet be blocked if unsupported claims reach customer-facing recommendations.

Long traces are useful for diagnosing why a claim failed, but the last dry run saw a 1.34x cost increase due to unchecked retries; finance accepted trace retention only if retries are capped and the run manifest includes retry count, estimated cost, and a short explanation of what the trace helped resolve.

Holdout cases should be visible in dashboards only as summaries/aggregate scores, while their labels/expected answers should stay out of prompt-iteration workflows to reduce risk of memorizing visible examples and to catch overfitting to demo cases.

A proposed path to expansion is to run one more visible batch before live orchestration, keeping citation support as a shipping gate, including retry counts in the manifest, and reporting holdout only in aggregate; expansion is framed as acceptable only if the next candidate improves grounding without exceeding the cost guardrail.

openQuestions: What specific threshold/criteria will define the citation-support shipping gate (beyond “unsupported claims” being a release block)? [S2]
What retry cap and cost guardrail values will be used to operationalize the finance constraint? [S3]
What dashboard clustering taxonomy and evidence format will be used to separate unsupported claims, weak citations, cost regressions, and holdout concerns as reviewers requested? [S5]
How many cases constitute the next “smaller visible batch,” and what constitutes sufficient pattern clustering to justify expanding coverage? [S1]
recommendation: Proceed with a limited expansion: run one more smaller, reviewer-friendly visible batch with failure clustering, then expand automated coverage only if grounding/citation support passes the explicit shipping gate and costs stay within the retry-capped guardrail with manifest reporting; keep holdout reporting aggregate-only and keep holdout labels out of tuning workflows.
